Real Analysis

Subsequences and Their Role in Convergence and Divergence

Subsequences and Their Role in Convergence and Divergence

Definitions and Notation

A subsequence picks out infinitely many terms of a sequence, in order, while discarding the rest. This idea will turn out to be exactly the right tool for both confirming convergence and, more usefully, for proving divergence — since a single “bad” subsequence is often easier to exhibit than to analyze the whole sequence directly.

Sequence and Subsequence
  • A real sequence is a function X:N→RX:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{R}X:N→R, written X=(xn)n∈NX=(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}X=(xn​)n∈N​ with xn=X(n)x_n = X(n)xn​=X(n).
  • A subsequence of XXX is any sequence of the form (xnk)k∈N(x_{n_k})_{k\in\mathbb{N}}(xnk​​)k∈N​ where (nk)k∈N(n_k)_{k\in\mathbb{N}}(nk​)k∈N​ is a strictly increasing sequence in N\mathbb{N}N (i.e. n1<n2<⋯n_1 < n_2 < \cdotsn1​<n2​<⋯).
  • Functionally, if A={nk:k∈N}⊂NA = \{n_k : k\in\mathbb{N}\} \subset \mathbb{N}A={nk​:k∈N}⊂N, then X∣A:A→RX|_A : A \to \mathbb{R}X∣A​:A→R is the restriction of XXX to AAA. Reindexing by k↦nkk \mapsto n_kk↦nk​ yields the subsequence (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​).
Even-Indexed Terms of (1/n)(1/n)(1/n)

Let xn=1nx_n = \frac{1}{n}xn​=n1​. The subsequence with even indices is

x2n=12n=12⋅1n,x_{2n} = \frac{1}{2n} = \tfrac12\cdot\frac{1}{n},x2n​=2n1​=21​⋅n1​,

i.e. 1/2, 1/4, 1/6, 1/8,…1/2,\ 1/4,\ 1/6,\ 1/8,\dots1/2, 1/4, 1/6, 1/8,…. Here (x2n)(x_{2n})(x2n​) happens to coincide termwise with the scaled sequence (12xn)\bigl(\tfrac12 x_n\bigr)(21​xn​) — but this is a coincidence of this particular example, not a general fact: a scaled sequence need not be a subsequence of the original.

Basic Property of Subsequences

The single most useful fact about subsequences is that they inherit convergence from the parent sequence. Intuitively, once the tail of (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is trapped near LLL, any subsequence — which only ever moves forward through the indices — is eventually trapped there too.

Subsequence of a Convergent Sequence

If xn→L∈Rx_n \to L \in \mathbb{R}xn​→L∈R, then every subsequence xnkx_{n_k}xnk​​ also converges to LLL.

Proof

Let ε>0\varepsilon > 0ε>0. Since xn→Lx_n \to Lxn​→L, there exists N∈NN \in \mathbb{N}N∈N such that ∣xn−L∣<ε|x_n - L| < \varepsilon∣xn​−L∣<ε for all n≥Nn \ge Nn≥N.

The index sequence (nk)(n_k)(nk​) is strictly increasing (hence unbounded in N\mathbb{N}N, by the Archimedean property), so there exists KKK such that nk≥Nn_k \ge Nnk​≥N for all k≥Kk \ge Kk≥K. Hence for all k≥Kk \ge Kk≥K, ∣xnk−L∣<ε|x_{n_k} - L| < \varepsilon∣xnk​​−L∣<ε. Therefore xnk→Lx_{n_k} \to Lxnk​​→L.

Taking the contrapositive of this theorem converts it into a divergence tool: exhibiting just one subsequence that misbehaves is enough to rule out convergence to LLL.

Contrapositive

If xn↛Lx_n \not\to Lxn​→L, then there exists a subsequence (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) that does not converge to LLL — indeed, one that stays at least some fixed distance from LLL infinitely often.

A Common Misreading to Avoid

The statement “if some subsequence of XXX converges to LLL, then xn→Lx_n \to Lxn​→L” is false. The correct direction is: if every subsequence of XXX converges to LLL, then xn→Lx_n \to Lxn​→L. A single well-behaved subsequence proves nothing about the whole sequence — this asymmetry is exactly what makes subsequences useful for disproving convergence but not, on their own, for proving it.

Characterizations of Non-Convergence to a Given Limit

The corollary above asserted that a “bad” subsequence exists when xn↛Lx_n \not\to Lxn​→L; we now make this precise by giving three logically equivalent ways to express non-convergence, moving from the raw negated definition to an explicit subsequence.

Equivalent Forms of Non-Convergence to LLL

Let X=(xn)X = (x_n)X=(xn​) be a sequence and L∈RL \in \mathbb{R}L∈R. The following are equivalent:

  1. xn↛Lx_n \not\to Lxn​→L.
  2. There exists ε0>0\varepsilon_0 > 0ε0​>0 such that for every k∈Nk \in \mathbb{N}k∈N there exists nk≥kn_k \ge knk​≥k with ∣xnk−L∣≥ε0|x_{n_k} - L| \ge \varepsilon_0∣xnk​​−L∣≥ε0​.
  3. There exist ε0>0\varepsilon_0 > 0ε0​>0 and a subsequence (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) of XXX such that ∣xnk−L∣≥ε0|x_{n_k} - L| \ge \varepsilon_0∣xnk​​−L∣≥ε0​ for all k∈Nk \in \mathbb{N}k∈N.
Proof

(1 ⇒ 2) Negating the definition of limit: since xn↛Lx_n \not\to Lxn​→L, there exists ε0>0\varepsilon_0 > 0ε0​>0 such that for every NNN there exists n≥Nn \ge Nn≥N with ∣xn−L∣≥ε0|x_n - L| \ge \varepsilon_0∣xn​−L∣≥ε0​. Taking N=kN = kN=k and choosing such n=nkn = n_kn=nk​ gives (2).

(2 ⇒ 3) Choose the indices in (2) successively beyond the previous one, so that (nk)(n_k)(nk​) is strictly increasing; the resulting (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) is a genuine subsequence satisfying ∣xnk−L∣≥ε0|x_{n_k}-L| \ge \varepsilon_0∣xnk​​−L∣≥ε0​ for all kkk.

(3 ⇒ 1) If xn→Lx_n \to Lxn​→L, the Subsequence Theorem above would force every subsequence — including (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) from (3) — to converge to LLL. But (3) gives a subsequence staying at least ε0\varepsilon_0ε0​ away from LLL, a contradiction.

Boundedness and Unboundedness

Before applying these ideas to divergence, we restate boundedness from the previous lecture and give its natural negation, phrased in a form that will feed directly into the construction below.

Bounded and Unbounded Sequences
  • Bounded: XXX is bounded if there exists M>0M > 0M>0 such that ∣xn∣≤M|x_n| \le M∣xn​∣≤M for all n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N.
  • Unbounded: XXX is unbounded if for every M>0M > 0M>0 there exists n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N with ∣xn∣>M|x_n| > M∣xn​∣>M. Equivalently, for each k∈Nk \in \mathbb{N}k∈N there exists nk∈Nn_k \in \mathbb{N}nk​∈N with ∣xnk∣>k|x_{n_k}| > k∣xnk​​∣>k.

From Unboundedness to a Reciprocal-Null Subsequence

Unboundedness lets us extract a subsequence that grows past every threshold kkk — and, just as importantly, we can always arrange the indices to be strictly increasing, so the result really is a subsequence.

Constructing a Rapidly Growing Subsequence

If X=(xn)X = (x_n)X=(xn​) is unbounded, then there exists a strictly increasing sequence (nk)(n_k)(nk​) such that

∣xnk∣>kfor all k∈N.|x_{n_k}| > k \quad\text{for all } k \in \mathbb{N}.∣xnk​​∣>kfor all k∈N.
Proof

By unboundedness, for k=1k=1k=1 choose n1n_1n1​ with ∣xn1∣>1|x_{n_1}| > 1∣xn1​​∣>1. Having chosen nkn_knk​, apply unboundedness again (with M=k+1M = k+1M=k+1, restricted to indices beyond nkn_knk​ — possible since only finitely many terms lie at or before nkn_knk​) to pick nk+1>nkn_{k+1} > n_knk+1​>nk​ with ∣xnk+1∣>k+1|x_{n_{k+1}}| > k+1∣xnk+1​​∣>k+1. This produces a strictly increasing (nk)(n_k)(nk​) with ∣xnk∣>k|x_{n_k}| > k∣xnk​​∣>k for every kkk.

The growth bound ∣xnk∣>k|x_{n_k}| > k∣xnk​​∣>k is exactly strong enough to force the reciprocal subsequence to vanish in the limit — a useful trick for showing sequences like 1/xn1/x_n1/xn​ converge even when (xn)(x_n)(xn​) itself does not.

Reciprocals Converge to 0

With (nk)(n_k)(nk​) as above, the subsequence (1/xnk)\bigl(1/x_{n_k}\bigr)(1/xnk​​) is well-defined and satisfies

lim⁡k→∞1xnk=0.\lim_{k\to\infty} \frac{1}{x_{n_k}} = 0.k→∞lim​xnk​​1​=0.
Proof

Since ∣xnk∣>k≥1|x_{n_k}| > k \ge 1∣xnk​​∣>k≥1, each xnk≠0x_{n_k} \neq 0xnk​​=0, so the reciprocal is defined. Then

∣1xnk∣≤1k.\left|\frac{1}{x_{n_k}}\right| \le \frac{1}{k}.​xnk​​1​​≤k1​.

Given ε>0\varepsilon > 0ε>0, choose K>1/εK > 1/\varepsilonK>1/ε (Archimedean property). For all k≥Kk \ge Kk≥K, ∣1xnk∣≤1k≤1K<ε\left|\frac{1}{x_{n_k}}\right| \le \frac1k \le \frac1K < \varepsilon​xnk​​1​​≤k1​≤K1​<ε.

Checklist for the Construction

To find such a subsequence explicitly, it suffices to arrange

∣xnk∣>kfor all k∈N,which implies0<1∣xnk∣<1k.|x_{n_k}| > k \quad \text{for all } k \in \mathbb{N}, \qquad\text{which implies}\qquad 0 < \frac{1}{|x_{n_k}|} < \frac1k.∣xnk​​∣>kfor all k∈N,which implies0<∣xnk​​∣1​<k1​.

This single growth condition is the only thing that needs verifying — everything else in the corollary above follows mechanically.

Sufficient Criteria for Divergence

The Contrapositive and Reciprocal corollaries above are really special cases of a general principle: subsequence behavior can certify divergence outright. We collect the two most common criteria here.

Divergence Criteria (Sufficient Conditions)

A sequence X=(xn)X = (x_n)X=(xn​) is divergent (has no finite limit) if at least one of the following holds:

  • there exist two subsequences xnkx_{n_k}xnk​​ and xmkx_{m_k}xmk​​ with different limits;
  • XXX is unbounded.
Why both criteria work

The first criterion is immediate from the Subsequence Theorem: if xn→Lx_n \to Lxn​→L, every subsequence converges to LLL, so two subsequences with different limits are incompatible with convergence. The second follows since every convergent sequence is bounded (Lecture 7) — an unbounded sequence cannot possibly converge.

Example: Geometric Sequences with 0<b<10 < b < 10<b<1

As a capstone example, we combine the Monotone Convergence Theorem (Lecture 8) with the Subsequence Theorem to pin down the limit of a geometric sequence — a case where monotonicity alone gives existence of a limit, but identifying its value requires a subsequence trick.

Limit of a Decaying Geometric Sequence

If 0<b<10 < b < 10<b<1, then

lim⁡n→∞bn=0.\lim_{n\to\infty} b^n = 0.n→∞lim​bn=0.
Proof

The sequence xn=bnx_n = b^nxn​=bn is decreasing (since 0<b<10<b<10<b<1) and bounded below by 000, hence convergent by the Monotone Convergence Theorem. Let lim⁡xn=x≥0\lim x_n = x \ge 0limxn​=x≥0.

Now (b2n)(b^{2n})(b2n) is a subsequence of (bn)(b^n)(bn) — taking indices nk=2kn_k = 2knk​=2k — so by the Subsequence Theorem it converges to the same limit xxx. But also b2n=(bn)2b^{2n} = (b^n)^2b2n=(bn)2, so by the Product Limit Law (Lecture 7), lim⁡b2n=x2\lim b^{2n} = x^2limb2n=x2.

Comparing the two expressions for this limit: x=x2x = x^2x=x2, so x(x−1)=0x(x-1) = 0x(x−1)=0, giving x∈{0,1}x \in \{0,1\}x∈{0,1}. Since 0<b<10 < b < 10<b<1, we have xn=bn≤b<1x_n = b^n \le b < 1xn​=bn≤b<1 for all n≥1n \ge 1n≥1, so x≤b<1x \le b < 1x≤b<1, ruling out x=1x = 1x=1. Therefore x=0x = 0x=0.

The subsequence trick as a general method

This proof illustrates a powerful technique: when a recursive or self-similar relationship exists between a sequence and one of its own subsequences (here, b2n=(bn)2b^{2n} = (b^n)^2b2n=(bn)2), the Subsequence Theorem lets us set up an algebraic equation for the limit — exactly as passing to the limit in a recursion did for the sequence xn+1=2xnx_{n+1}=\sqrt{2x_n}xn+1​=2xn​​ in Lecture 8. Monotone Convergence guarantees the limit exists; the subsequence relation then pins down its value.
